critique.summary: Nightingale is an open-source karaoke application that allows users to turn any song on their computer into a karaoke experience by separating vocals, transcribing lyrics with word-level synchronization, and providing pitch scoring. It supports multiple platforms including Linux, macOS, and Windows, and offers features such as player profiles, video file support, and gamepad navigation.
mainPoints: Nightingale uses UVR Karaoke or Demucs models to isolate vocals from instrumentals.
Lyrics are transcribed and aligned at the word level using WhisperX, with existing lyrics sourced from LRCLIB when available.
The app provides real-time pitch scoring and star ratings to track singing performance.
Supports multiple player profiles with separate score histories.
Can handle video files by extracting audio and playing the original video as background.
Includes dynamic GPU-accelerated backgrounds and gamepad support for navigation.
Runs on Linux (x86_64 and ARM), macOS (ARM and Intel), and Windows (x86_64) with GPU acceleration when available.
The app is self-contained with all dependencies bootstrapped on first launch, requiring no separate installation.
critique.truthfulness: The claims made in the article are consistent with publicly available information about Nightingale on its official website and related open-source project pages. The described features such as vocal separation using UVR Karaoke and Demucs, transcription with WhisperX, and multi-platform support are accurate and verifiable. The app's ability to handle video files and provide pitch scoring aligns with known functionalities of similar karaoke software. Overall, the article provides an accurate and truthful overview of the Nightingale app without unsupported or exaggerated claims.
considerations: The article does not mention any limitations or potential performance issues on lower-end hardware or without GPU acceleration.
There is no discussion of privacy or data security related to microphone input or user profiles.
The article does not specify the accuracy or language support range of the WhisperX transcription model.
No information is provided about the licensing terms or community involvement in the open-source project.
The article lacks user reviews or comparisons with other karaoke software to contextualize Nightingale's strengths or weaknesses.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the Nightingale open-source karaoke app, highlighting its features like local processing, pitch scoring, and open-source nature. Users express excitement, ask technical questions about functionality and compatibility, and raise concerns about virus warnings.

comments.author: rzzzzru
comments.text:
I've been working on a karaoke app called Nightingale. You point it at your music folder and it turns your songs into karaoke - separates vocals from instrumentals, generates word-level synced lyrics, and lets you sing with highlighted lyrics and pitch scoring. Works with video files too.

Everything runs locally on your machine, nothing gets uploaded. No accounts, no subscriptions, no telemetry.

It ships as a single binary for Linux, macOS, and Windows. On first launch it sets up its own isolated Python environment and downloads the ML models it needs - no manual installation of dependencies required.

My two biggest drivers for the creation of this were:

    The lack of karaoke coverage for niche, avant-garde, and local tracks.

    Nostalgia for the good old cheesy karaoke backgrounds with flowing rivers, city panoramas, etc.
Some highlights:

    Stem separation using the UVR Karaoke model (preserves backing vocals) or Demucs

    Automatic lyrics via WhisperX transcription, or fetched from LRCLIB when available

    Pitch scoring with player profiles and scoreboards

    Gamepad support and TV-friendly UI scaling for party setups

    GPU acceleration on NVIDIA (CUDA) and Apple Silicon (CoreML/MPS)

    Built with Rust and the Bevy engine
The whole stack is open source. No premium tier, no "open core" - just the app.

Feedback and contributions welcome.
comments.analysis: The developer provides a detailed and technical description of the app's features and implementation, which is credible and informative.

comments.author: dmd
comments.text: This looks great, but I don't understand what it's supposed to do. I assumed the idea was "remove the lyrics" but of the 5 songs I tried (from Cry Cry Cry, Indigo Girls, and Suzanne Vega), none seemed to have any change from the original at all - it's showing the words on the screen (and the timing is perfect) but it's not removing the singing at all. How do you turn off the singing?
comments.analysis: This user provides a practical test and reports an issue with vocal removal, offering useful feedback that highlights a potential limitation or misunderstanding.

comments.author: Eckter2
comments.text:
Very nice!

I've worked on a small toy project with a similar purpose in the past [1], though it's not nearly as polished as yours, and I've made some questionable decisions here and there.

I have questions about pitch tracking. It seems you do track the pitch for scoring, and there's a line at the top of the screen that seems related but that I can't figure out. For my use case, an important feature of karaoke apps is displaying how "high" the next note should be sung, or at least some hints. Is it something your app can do and I just haven't figured it out? Or would it be a feature request?

[1] https://github.com/eckter/karaoke_helper
comments.analysis: The commenter provides relevant experience and asks insightful questions about pitch tracking features, contributing constructively to the discussion.

comments.author: upsuper
comments.text:
Really nice project, I'm looking forward to trying it!

Would it be possible to process songs on one device, and then use the result in another, or even multiple? Or would it be possible to run as separate server / client?

I ask mainly because the device I connect to my TV is definitely not the most powerful one, so it would be nice if I can preprocess the songs elsewhere.
comments.analysis: This comment raises practical questions about app deployment and performance optimization, reflecting thoughtful engagement with the project.

comments.author: mynameajeff
comments.text: My wife is a huge karaoke fan. I'm especially interested in the pitch scoring since we usually play the karaoke games on older consoles for that exact feature. Nobody really makes games like that anymore without a subscription (and most of these good modern karaoke platforms are exclusive to east asia anyways). If this works well this could make for some really fun social events, looking forward to trying this.
comments.analysis: The user shares personal context and interest in pitch scoring, providing a relatable perspective on the app's potential social impact.

comments.author: arvinsim
comments.text: I am getting a virus detected error when downloading from Chrome.
comments.analysis: This comment reports a security concern that may affect user trust, though it lacks detail on the nature of the detection.

comments.author: caipira
comments.text: This looks amazing! looking forward to test it on the weekend. Does it work well on a raspberry pi with 4 gigs of ram?
comments.analysis: The user expresses enthusiasm and asks a relevant question about hardware compatibility, which is important for potential users.

comments.author: reddalo
comments.text:
Looks amazing. I've been using Karafun [1], a paid service, but this seems promising.

[1] https://www.karafun.com/
comments.analysis: This comment compares Nightingale to an existing paid service, providing useful context for evaluating the new app.

comments.author: hasbot
comments.text:
I hate to be that guy that points out other projects but YARG has vocals and there are a slew of songs that can be used.

https://www.yarg.in

https://www.enchor.us/?&hasVocals=true
comments.analysis: The commenter provides alternative resources, which adds value to the discussion by broadening the options for users.

comments.author: ETlol
comments.text: VirusTotal says the .EXE is flagged by 1 security vendor but threat is low
comments.analysis: This comment provides a measured security assessment, helping to clarify the virus detection concern raised by another user.
